module_0.title: EU AI Act Standards Vacuum persists ahead of high-risk obligations
module_0.body: No harmonised standards published in Official Journal as of Q1 2026, with high-risk AI obligations due August 2026. CEN-CENELEC drafts in progress but compliance gap remains active, creating regulatory uncertainty for providers. GPAI Code of Practice first release published, due for full finalisation Q2 2026. Watch for CEN-CENELEC updates and potential enforcement delays.

mainstream.headline: EU AI Act Standards Vacuum active pre-high-risk deadline
mainstream.why_it_matters: Ongoing absence of Official Journal standards complicates compliance for high-risk systems due August 2026, risking fragmented national implementations and enforcement challenges across Member States.

mainstream.headline: GPAI Code of Practice first release published
mainstream.why_it_matters: Initial version provides interim guidance for general-purpose AI models, with full finalisation pending Q2 2026, shaping transparency and systemic risk obligations for frontier providers.

mainstream.headline: EU AI Act entered force August 2024 with phased rollout
mainstream.why_it_matters: Provisions apply gradually through 2026-2027, establishing risk-based framework as global benchmark despite implementation frictions.

underweighted.headline: CEN-CENELEC JTC 21 drafts underway without OJ publication
underweighted.why_it_matters: Standards development lags essential requirements timeline, enabling definitional arbitrage for non-standard-compliant systems and pressuring EU AI Office coordination.

underweighted.headline: EDPS designated as MSA for EU institutions AI systems
underweighted.why_it_matters: Expands oversight to internal EU bodies, creating dual role as notified body for high-risk conformity, but risks resource constraints in multi-level supervision.

models.name: Gemini 3D
models.headline: Interactive 3D models in chatbot responses
models.asymmetric: Advances multimodal interfaces but no disclosed safety evals for generated simulations

models.name: Muse Spark
models.headline: Multimodal model for apps and devices
models.asymmetric: Ecosystem integration accelerates T3 deployment without noted governance protocols

models.name: GLM-5.1
models.headline: Open-source for long-horizon engineering tasks
models.asymmetric: MIT license enables unrestricted agentic use cases lacking safety guardrails

funding_rounds.investors: SoftBank, Amazon ($50B total), Nvidia ($30B), MGX (UAE), a16z, D.E. Shaw, first retail tranche ($3B)
funding_rounds.asymmetric: Retail tranche opening marks transition from venture to quasi-public infrastructure financing. At $852B vs $14B+ revenue, aggressive monetisation — price-cutting on APIs, government contract expansion — is structurally required.

funding_rounds.investors: AON Consulting (lead), Tom Brady, Joe Lonsdale (8VC), Linda Yaccarino
funding_rounds.asymmetric: AON is the world’s largest benefits broker — distribution-as-investment. Channels Fortune 500 employer health plan dollars into an AI-native platform it can resell through its own benefits consulting relationships.

funding_rounds.investors: Eclipse, Factory, Menlo Ventures, Prosperity7 Ventures (Saudi Aramco), Triatomic Capital
funding_rounds.asymmetric: Prosperity7 (Saudi Aramco’s venture arm) investing in AI agent infrastructure signals sovereign commodity wealth hedging against AI disruption of the energy sector by owning the infrastructure layer.

funding_rounds.investors: Grove Ventures, M&G Investments, PSG Equity, Meitav
funding_rounds.asymmetric: M&G Investments (UK insurance/asset management) signals European institutional capital seeking critical infrastructure AI exposure ahead of EU AI Act deployment mandates.

strategic_deals.amount: Maven elevated to department-wide program of record; Army contracts approaching $10B over 10 years
strategic_deals.asymmetric: Program of record designation locks in multi-decade revenue with switching costs that dwarf the contract values. No other commercial AI platform has this status.
Compute supply increasingly locked into bilateral agreements between hyperscalers and chip designers. Concentrates compute access further and creates structural moats for the signatories.

energy_wall.deal: EIA pilot survey on data center energy use
energy_wall.analysis: EIA launched voluntary surveys targeting 196 data center operators across Texas, Washington State, and Northern Virginia/DC. First systematic federal effort to characterise AI data center energy consumption. Once baseline data collected, mandatory reporting frameworks typically follow within 18 months.

Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI, Oracle, xAI signed White House pledge to build, bring, or buy dedicated power and pay full transmission costs. Converts Big Seven from technology companies into entities with regulated-utility-style obligations.

energy_wall.signal: CONFIRMED — ends invisible hyperscale loads in highest-density US AI corridor
energy_wall.analysis: PJM filed to limit legacy netting for behind-the-meter loads >50 MW. New constructs (Connect-and-Manage, BYOG) require large AI data centers to participate explicitly in regional capacity markets. Northern Virginia affected first.

headline_developments: Qualified Health $125M Series B (25 March) — Anthropic embedded as infrastructure partner via Anthology Fund; 7% of US hospital revenue covered.
eMed $200M at $2B+ (26 March) — AON Consulting-led AI telehealth platform embedding into employer benefits distribution.
FDA deregulation: single-recommendation AI devices no longer require FDA review under Commissioner Makary.
ARISE Network: 95% of FDA-cleared AI medical devices have never reported a single patient health outcome.
sectors.capability_to_deployment_gap: FDA deregulation widens the gap by removing the pre-market evidence bar. AI diagnostic tools clearing regulatory thresholds while nearly none validated for real-world patient outcomes.
sectors.stealth_flag: UnitedHealth Group (Optum Insight) quietly ramping AI to automate prior authorisation and denial workflows before provider-side AI can catch up on appeals — asymmetric deployment entrenching payer advantages.
sectors.asymmetric: FDA deregulation + Anthropic health system embedding + CMS ACCESS Model (July 2026) creates 90-day window for AI health platforms to lock in partnerships before payment frameworks crystallise. Companies with existing enterprise contracts will shape how CMS structures AI reimbursement codes.

headline_developments: Harvey $200M at $11B — GIC (Singapore sovereign wealth) co-leading signals legal AI as strategic economic infrastructure.
2026 SKILLS Survey: AI in production at 130 largest law firms across 7 substantive use-case categories. Pilot phase over.
Legora $550M Series D at $5.55B — US expansion into Houston and Chicago from Swedish base.
sectors.capability_to_deployment_gap: Narrowing rapidly at large firms. Gap now lives at mid-market (50–500 lawyers). Corporate legal departments demanding (not just accepting) AI tools from outside counsel.
sectors.stealth_flag: Quinn Emanuel developed AI platform for real-time trial querying of discovery data — AI embedded in the actual trial process, without regulatory scrutiny.
sectors.asymmetric: Corporate legal departments will stop treating legal AI as an experiment and require outside counsel to demonstrate measurable efficiency gains. Economic pressure flows from buyer (corporate) to seller (firms).

sectors.status: Accelerating (adoption near-universal; differentiation shifting to governance)
headline_developments: Finastra: 98% AI adoption in financial services globally. Only 2% report no AI use.
Agentic AI in core banking: Eurobank, Wells Fargo, Metro Bank embedded agentic AI in transaction monitoring and compliance.
Colorado AI Act enforcement deadline: June 30, 2026.
sectors.capability_to_deployment_gap: Small in risk/fraud/compliance; larger in agentic customer-facing applications constrained by model risk management requirements.
sectors.stealth_flag: Vietnam leads globally at 74% active AI deployment in financial services — regulatory-light jurisdiction generating AI financial services deployment data at pace that will pressure Western regulators.
sectors.asymmetric: State-level AI acts (Colorado June 2026, Texas RAIGA) + White House federal preemption recommendation creates 90-day regulatory arbitrage window for institutions that locked in deployments before state deadlines.

headline_developments: Palantir Maven elevated to department-wide program of record (22 March).
OpenAI-Pentagon deal amended (3 March) — surveillance red lines added after backlash.
German Army publicly committed to deploying AI for battlefield analysis using Ukrainian combat data.
Shield AI $2B at $12.7B — institutional capital (JPMorgan, Blackstone) formalising defence AI as asset class.
sectors.capability_to_deployment_gap: Narrow for ISR and targeting support; wide for autonomous weapons where human-in-the-loop policy constraints remain intentionally maintained.
sectors.stealth_flag: xAI/SpaceX merger creates single entity combining AI model (Grok), satellite connectivity (Starlink), and launch capacity — an AI-enabled space-based ISR platform controlled by one private actor with direct presidential access, without regulatory oversight framework.
sectors.asymmetric: Anthropic supply-chain risk designation establishes that the executive branch can exclude AI companies from defense contracts based on safety policy disagreements. AI safety stances are now market-access risks, not merely reputational considerations.

headline_developments: Reuters Institute: Google search traffic to publishers down ~33% year-over-year. 43% forecast >75% decline within 3 years.
Breaking news traffic up 103% — AI-resistant category where editorial judgment has comparative advantage.
Muck Rack: 82% of journalists use at least one AI tool; disinformation concern up to 26%.
sectors.capability_to_deployment_gap: Near-zero for AI tooling in newsroom workflows; substantial for AI-as-publisher.
sectors.stealth_flag: Washington Post AI-generated personalised podcast produced more content in three days than in its entire previous history — AI-authored journalism at scale running on a brand trusted for human editorial work.
sectors.asymmetric: OpenAI wound Sora down to redirect compute to next major model, putting $1B Disney deal on ice. AI-media content partnerships are fragile at infrastructure level — a single compute reallocation can void a major IP deal. Media companies should treat AI partnerships as contingent revenue.

headline_developments: FutureEd 2026 tracker: 53 bills across 25 states.
Ohio HB 96: mandatory district AI policies by July 1, 2026 for all 608 Ohio school districts.
EdSurge: dominant teacher adoption mode is AI for instructional content generation.
sectors.capability_to_deployment_gap: Large. Gap between what students do with AI outside school and what is sanctioned inside is widening.
sectors.stealth_flag: White House federal preemption recommendation, if enacted, voids most stringent student data protections before they take effect. Ed-tech vendors lobbying for federal preemption gain market access without complying with strictest privacy standards.
sectors.asymmetric: Ohio July 1 deadline creates predictable procurement wave for 608 school districts. Similar deadlines in Idaho, Utah, Maryland create regional procurement acceleration by statutory calendar.

headline_developments: EIA launches pilot survey targeting 196 data center operators (25 March) — mandatory reporting precursor.
DHS plans AI-ISAC for critical infrastructure.
AMD $100B Meta GPU deal; Deutsche Telekom/Nvidia €1B Munich AI data center opened; Adani $100B AI infrastructure plan.
sectors.capability_to_deployment_gap: For power infrastructure: minimal — hyperscaler capital deploying faster than grid capacity. For AI in OT systems: large gap driven by appropriate caution. CISA guidance emphasises fail-safe mechanisms.
sectors.stealth_flag: PJM Connect-and-Manage service construct means largest AI data centers could be first loads cut during grid stress — an AI model training run being curtailed mid-operation is a new category of operational risk not yet priced in SLAs.
sectors.asymmetric: Ratepayer Protection Pledge + PJM rulemaking + EIA survey = three-vector convergence toward treating hyperscalers as regulated utilities. Within 18 months, AI data center operators may face utility-style rate regulation and ROI caps.

european.item: EU AI Office: Code of Practice on AI content labelling consultation closed 30 March
european.summary: The EU Commission’s voluntary Code of Practice on Article 50 transparency (marking/labelling of AI-generated content) closed public consultation on the final day of the reporting window. Finalisation expected by June 2026. If delivery slips, Article 50 operators face a standard-less compliance period at August 2 go-live — a transparency domain standards vacuum.
european.asymmetric: The June finalisation clock is now running. If the code is not in place before August 2, the transparency mandate activates without the implementing guidance that was supposed to operationalise it.

european.item: EU AI Act Omnibus trilogue formally opened
european.summary: European Parliament plenary voted 569–45–23 to adopt negotiating position. Trilogue with Council begins April 2026. Critical path: agreement 28 April → Parliament vote June → OJ publication July → entry into force 1 August. EP and Council diverge on transparency grace period (EP: 3 months; Council: 6 months). Both agreed on December 2027 / August 2028 high-risk deadlines.
european.standards_vacuum: ACTIVE. If trilogue slips past April 28, Omnibus cannot enter force before 2 August 2026 — original high-risk obligations apply on 2 August without the Omnibus’s stop-the-clock protection.
european.asymmetric: The 28 April target is the entire risk management window for Europe’s AI ecosystem. A single failed trilogue session can create the compliance cliff.

european.item: CEN-CENELEC JTC21: prEN 18281 advances to Stage 40 (Enquiry)
european.summary: prEN 18281 (Evaluation Methods for Accurate Computer Vision Systems) advanced to Stage 40 (public enquiry). prEN 18283 advanced to Stage 20. Standards Vacuum ACTIVE — no harmonised standard yet cited in OJ; 124 days to compliance deadline.
european.standards_vacuum_detail: Most advanced standard (prEN 18281) is one of ~7 tracked; majority remain at Stage 20–30. Publication, Commission assessment, and OJ citation all follow enquiry. Arithmetically impossible to close the vacuum before 2 August regardless of Omnibus outcome.

china.item: Ciyuan — carry forward; corporate confirmation active
china.summary: No new state-level speech in window. Carry forward unchanged. Corporate confirmation: Alibaba Token Hub restructuring + Chinese AI cloud providers (Alibaba, Baidu, Zhipu) all announcing price increases from mid-April 2026 citing demand exceeding supply. Ciyuan token economy propagating from state to corporate.
china.asymmetric: Chinese labs executing coordinated price increases signals market power consolidation, not distress. Structural shift from 2025’s race-to-zero pricing.

china.item: DeepSeek V4 — still pending; Huawei-first hardware embargo confirmed
china.summary: DeepSeek V4 not released as of 1 April 2026. Chinese media points to April 2026 window. Known: 1M token context, Engram Memory architecture, optimised for Huawei Ascend (withheld from Nvidia/AMD per Reuters 27 February). If DeepSeek V4 drops during EU Omnibus trilogue (April), it will land at Europe’s most sensitive regulatory decision point.
china.asymmetric: Withheld from US chip suppliers = deliberate strategy to maintain competitive information advantage and accelerate Huawei Ascend commercial validation. Timing of April release (if it occurs) vs. 28 April Omnibus target is a notable geopolitical overlap.

china.item: China’s 15th Five-Year Plan — AI Plus as top national priority; 52 AI mentions vs. 11 in 14th plan
china.summary: NPC finalised plan (5 March). Target: 90% AI penetration in key sectors by 2030. USCC Working Paper ‘Two Loops’ (23 March) confirmed Chinese open-source AI strategy reinforcing industrial dominance. Chinese open-weight models surpassed US models in weekly token call volume (4.12T vs. 2.94T, February 2026).
china.asymmetric: US export controls targeting training compute do not address the physical loop of deployment-driven industrial data accumulation. Policy framework misaligned with actual competitive frontier.

china.item: Zhipu GLM-5.1 released (27 March) — 94.6% of Claude Opus 4.6 on coding benchmarks
china.summary: 744B MoE (40B active parameters), MIT licensed. Zhipu simultaneously raised API price by 20%. Weekly Chinese model token volume surpassed US models in February 2026.

threshold_events.title: AI independently rediscovered the Standard Model of particle physics from raw 1950s–60s data
threshold_events.summary: Unsupervised ML applied to raw particle physics experimental data recovered baryon number, isospin, strangeness, charm, the Eightfold Way multiplet structure, and Regge trajectories — the core organising principles of the Standard Model — without any theoretical prior knowledge. Published in Journal of High Energy Physics 31 March.
reliability_flags: Standard ML methods (PCA, t-SNE, clustering); no novel architecture
Mirrors earlier work recovering the periodic table from chemical data
threshold_events.asymmetric: AI can be deployed as a systematic scanner over bodies of experimental data to identify previously unrecognised patterns. Next steps: can AI recover quark substructure? infer gauge symmetries? Points toward methodological shift in hypothesis-generation for fundamental physics.

threshold_events.title: Neuro-symbolic AI autonomously solved open cosmic string power spectrum problem
threshold_events.summary: A neuro-symbolic system (Gemini Deep Think + Tree Search + automated numerical feedback) derived novel exact analytical solutions for the power spectrum of gravitational radiation from cosmic strings — a previously open problem in theoretical physics. No human guidance in the derivation.
threshold_events.asymmetric: Autonomous derivation of new exact solutions to open mathematical physics problems. Combined with the NYU AD Standard Model result, March 2026 marks AI demonstrating autonomous capability across the full stack of mathematical and physical discovery.

programme_updates.lab: Google DeepMind / EMBL-EBI / NVIDIA / Seoul National University
programme_updates.update: 1.7 million high-confidence homodimer predictions added (March 2026) — first expansion to include protein complexes. 30M total calculated. 18M lower-confidence homodimers available for bulk download. Heterodimers in analysis pipeline. Changes functional scope from single proteins to how proteins interact — directly relevant to AMR drug discovery.

GPT-5.4 Thinking (5 March) is the first general-purpose model rated HIGH in Cybersecurity — 73.33% combined pass rate on CTF, CVE-Bench, Cyber Range. HIGH in Biological & Chemical also. No Critical tier for any model. GPT-5.4 mini: Below High across all categories.

RSP v3.0 (24 February 2026) active. Current ASL: ASL-3. No tier change. 24 March: minor noncompliance reporting policy update expanding employee reporting channels. Pause commitment removed. ASL-4+ moved to ‘industry recommendations’.

AlphaFold: major protein complex expansion (see threshold events). AlphaGenome: API live (non-commercial), no new window updates. AlphaEvolve: active in production (data center scheduling, TPU design, Gemini training), no new release. WeatherNext 2: live in Google products, no window update. GNoME: automated lab in preparation, no window update.

arxiv_highlights.title: Reward Hacking as Structural Equilibrium (arXiv:2603.28063)
arxiv_highlights.significance: Proves reward hacking is inescapable structural equilibrium; derives computable distortion index predicting hacking severity before deployment. Evaluation coverage → zero as tool count grows in agentic systems.

arxiv_highlights.title: EU AI Act Article 50 II Structural Compliance Gaps (arXiv:2603.26983)
arxiv_highlights.significance: Three structural compliance gaps in EU AI Act transparency mandate cannot be addressed through post-hoc labelling before August 2026 deadline.

drill_down_results.title: AI productivity amplification in scientific research
drill_down_results.summary: Scientists using AI tools published ~33% more papers on arXiv; >50% more on bioRxiv/SSRN. Largest effect for non-native English speakers. 4,475 cs.AI papers in March 2026 alone. AI agents autonomously doing AI research on AI models (arXiv:2603.28015: 3,106 autonomous experiments on molecular modelling architecture search).
drill_down_results.asymmetric: Volume (4,475 papers/month) and self-referential nature of AI research on AI will stress peer review infrastructure and compress regulatory impact assessment timelines.

vectors.summary: No harmonised standards in Official Journal ahead of high-risk obligations August 2026.
vectors.asymmetric: Enables market entry via national workarounds, fragmenting single market goals.

vectors.summary: Phased rollout strains multi-level supervision with AI Office and national authorities.
vectors.asymmetric: GPAI extraterritoriality tests enforcement against US labs.

vectors.summary: Risk-based categories create gaps for GPAI and open-source models.
vectors.asymmetric: Sparse architectures may evade compute thresholds.

vectors.summary: National MSAs and EU AI Board face coordination challenges.
vectors.asymmetric: Resource gaps in EDPS dual role amplify institutional risks.

vectors.summary: Sandboxes mitigate but high-risk rules burden SMEs.
vectors.asymmetric: Regulatory burden shifts power to compliant incumbents.

vectors.summary: AI Act extraterritoriality collides with US/China frameworks.
vectors.asymmetric: Sets precedent for standards contest in ISO/IEC.

items.title: OpenAI-Pentagon classified deployment deal (amended 3 March 2026)
items.summary: OpenAI deployed models on Pentagon’s classified network. Amendment added prohibition on surveillance of US persons and explicit ‘human responsibility for use of force’ red line. OpenAI engineers forward-deployed with safety researchers in the loop.
items.ihl_friction: Amendment covers use of force, not the broader lethal decision support chain (targeting analysis, pattern-of-life). ‘Any lawful purpose’ baseline still permits uses short of direct kinetic action that could facilitate IHL violations. No independent oversight mechanism confirmed.
items.asymmetric: OpenAI’s outcome (deal preserved, red lines contractual) vs. Anthropic’s (contract terminated, litigation required) creates precedent: rewarding contractual compliance over public confrontation.

items.title: Anthropic Pentagon injunction — supply chain risk designation blocked (26 March 2026)
items.summary: Federal judge found Pentagon’s designation of Anthropic as supply chain risk was ‘classic illegal First Amendment retaliation.’ DoD records showed designation issued because of Anthropic’s ‘hostile manner through the press.’ DC Circuit challenge still pending. Sen. Gillibrand letter reveals DoD rejected vendor request to memorialise restriction on fully autonomous weapons use.
items.ihl_friction: DoD’s AI Strategy explicitly silent on governance, legal oversight, test, evaluation, and validation safeguards per Gillibrand letter. Rejection of guardrails creates structural IHL compliance gap for autonomous weapons supply chain.
items.asymmetric: Creates structural deterrent against other labs maintaining stated safety commitments in government contracts. Every frontier AI lab must now calculate how its safety stance affects government contract eligibility.

items.title: Pentagon FY2026 AI budget: $14.2B for AI and autonomous systems
items.summary: Record budget request. Replicator program (expendable autonomous drones/vessels) received $1B in FY2025. FY2026 NDAA authorised $900.6B total defence spending with AI reporting requirements but no new autonomous weapons restrictions. US and Russia voted against November 2025 UN resolution calling for legally enforceable LAWS agreement.
items.ihl_friction: 120+ countries endorsed calls for new treaty on AWS. Three largest military AI developers (US, Russia, China) oppose binding restrictions. CCW GGE highly unlikely to produce legally binding protocol by 2026 deadline.
items.asymmetric: IHL accountability gap: IHL built around human decisions cannot easily attribute violations to autonomous systems.

law_highlights.title: EU AI Act Omnibus trilogue opened — 28 April agreement target
law_highlights.summary: Parliament 569–45–23. Council position adopted 13 March. Critical path: 28 April agreement → June Parliament vote → July OJ publication → 1 August entry into force. If any step slips, original high-risk obligations apply on 2 August without Omnibus stop-the-clock.

law_highlights.title: White House National AI Policy Framework — federal preemption of state AI laws
law_highlights.summary: Nonbinding framework calls for federal preemption of state AI laws, no new federal AI regulator, sector-specific enforcement. Explicitly endorses court resolution of copyright/fair-use questions. Explicitly prohibits states from regulating AI model development. DOJ AI Litigation Task Force evaluation of ‘onerous’ state laws overdue.

law_highlights.title: US state AI legislation surge — 80+ active bills
law_highlights.summary: Transparency Coalition documents active AI bills in 15+ states. Colorado AI Act enforcement: June 30, 2026. Multiple bills target companion chatbots, deepfakes, healthcare AI, and employment discrimination.

standards_highlights.title: EU AI Act 7-Layer Tracker — updated
standards_highlights.summary: L1 (Prohibited practices): Applied since Feb 2025. L2 (GPAI): Applied since Aug 2025; enforcement Aug 2026. L3 (High-risk Annex III): Pending Aug 2026; may shift to Dec 2027 if Omnibus adopted. L4 (Annex I products): Pending Aug 2027/2028. L5 (Omnibus): In trilogue; target April 28. L6 (Standards): VACUUM ACTIVE — no harmonised standards in OJ; 122 days to deadline. L7 (NCAs): Building; member state sandbox deadline Aug 2026.

standards_highlights.title: CEN-CENELEC JTC21: prEN 18281 advances to Stage 40
standards_highlights.summary: Computer vision evaluation standard (prEN 18281) entered public enquiry phase. prEN 18283 at Stage 20. No harmonised standard has reached Published/Operational status. Arithmetically impossible to close the vacuum before 2 August even if enquiry vote positive.

litigation_highlights.title: Anthropic v. Department of Defense — preliminary injunction granted (26 March 2026)
litigation_highlights.summary: California federal judge found Pentagon supply-chain risk designation constituted ‘classic illegal First Amendment retaliation.’ DoD records showed designation issued due to Anthropic’s ‘hostile manner through the press.’ DC Circuit challenge on statutory authority under 10 U.S.C. §3252 still pending.

litigation_highlights.title: AI copyright litigation — Meta summary judgment on fair use granted
litigation_highlights.summary: Federal judge granted Meta summary judgment on fair use grounds in books case. Court found use highly transformative; no evidence of market dilution. OpenAI-trade-secrets lawsuit dismissed for lack of detail. Class certification timelines extend to March 2027.

litigation_highlights.title: FTC AI enforcement: Rytr vacatur and deregulatory pivot
litigation_highlights.summary: FTC vacated 2024 Rytr consent order under Trump AI Action Plan. Section 5 enforcement narrowing to false advertising/AI washing rather than capability-based preemptive prohibition. FTC chatbot investigation for child protection ongoing.

international_soft_law.item: OECD AI-WIPS 2026 Conference (exact window: 30 March – 1 April)
international_soft_law.summary: 6th OECD AI in Work conference across exact AGM window. Sessions on agentic AI governance, algorithmic management (89% of firms have at least one governance measure; effectiveness uncertain), AI capability indicators for occupations, and AI in social protection. OECD’s AI Capability Indicators framework — first results presented — is the most credible multilateral data infrastructure for tracking labour market displacement. No new binding multilateral instruments.

international_soft_law.item: Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI — lacks US and China
international_soft_law.summary: First legally binding international AI treaty opened September 2024. GPPi (26 March) confirms it lacks US and China signatures. Structural governance limitation confirmed.

corporate_governance.item: OpenAI Pentagon deal as RSP stress test
corporate_governance.summary: Original deal permitted ‘any lawful use.’ Red lines only added after public backlash via amendment. Anthropic’s attempt to enforce identical red lines resulted in supply chain risk designation and litigation. 37 OpenAI and DeepMind employees filed amicus brief warning punitive measures could chill public discussion of AI risks.

product_liability.item: EU AI Act August 2026 general application — compliance cliff risk
product_liability.summary: General application date: 2 August 2026. Omnibus aims to delay Annex III high-risk to December 2027. CIOs in regulatory limbo. No first EU AI strict liability case filed. Standards Vacuum means notified bodies cannot certify high-risk systems regardless of Omnibus outcome.

algorithmic_accountability.item: GSA AI procurement clause — ‘any lawful’ + irrevocable license
algorithmic_accountability.summary: Comment period closed April 3. Grants irrevocable ‘any lawful’ use rights; prohibits inference-level output refusals; bars non-US AI systems. GSA used MAS solicitation refresh not 60-day Federal Register notice — procedural challenge grounds under 41 U.S.C. §1707.

governance_gaps.gap: AISI leadership vacuum approaching August 2026 enforcement deadline
governance_gaps.analysis: UK AISI on interim leadership since October 2025 (permanent director search ongoing). US NIST AI Safety Institute restructured and capacity-reduced to CAISI. EU AI Office: no new senior appointments confirmed. All three principal government AI safety bodies in leadership transition or reduced capacity precisely as EU AI Act enforcement deadline (2 August 2026) and GPAI enforcement approach. Creates international safety governance coordination vacuum at the most consequential moment since the Paris AI Action Summit.

White House Framework opposes new federal AI regulator. GSA clause attempts to codify ‘any lawful’ as procurement standard. Pentagon used supply chain risk designation as political retaliation. Governance is being conducted through procurement mechanisms that bypass democratic oversight, judicial review, and notice-and-comment rulemaking.

items.title: OpenAI Pentagon deal — stated principles vs. contract reality
items.accountability_friction: Stated commitment (surveillance red line, human control of force) materialised only after public pressure required amendment. Original deal’s ‘any lawful use’ standard accepted without stated red lines. No independent audit or enforcement mechanism for compliance with AI safeguards in classified environments. Gap score: HIGH. Classified deployment = no public verification possible.

items.title: Anthropic blacklisted for enforcing its stated policies
items.accountability_friction: Unusual direction: Anthropic’s principles were consistent with its actions (refusing to surrender guardrails) and it was penalised for that consistency. The accountability gap belongs to the contracting authority. Court’s preliminary injunction confirms designation was retaliatory. Creates structural deterrent against other labs maintaining safety commitments in government contracts.

items.title: ACM ethics community diagnoses ‘principle fatigue’
items.accountability_friction: ACM USTPC: well-intentioned AI ethics declarations without enforcement have failed to prevent real-world harms. Shift to agentic AI increases friction: ethics frameworks built around AI ‘decisions’ do not map onto AI ‘actions’ (agent sequences), creating responsibility attribution vacuum at the exact moment AI systems are most consequential.

items.title: FTC deregulatory pivot: Rytr vacatur signals higher bar for AI enforcement
items.accountability_friction: FTC vacated Rytr consent order (mass-produced fake reviews) for insufficient evidence of ‘tangible consumer harm.’ Proactive capability-based enforcement dismantled while AI-enabled fraud scales. Gap: evidence bar now high enough to permit significant AI-enabled harm before enforcement triggers.

module_12.title: Information Operations

items.title: EEAS 4th FIMI Report: AI in 25% of incidents; FIMI Deterrence Playbook introduced
items.region: Global; primary: Ukraine, France, Moldova, Germany
items.platform_response: FIMI Deterrence Playbook — targets infrastructure/supply chain of FIMI actors rather than individual incidents
items.detection_method: EEAS FIMI Explorer real-time dashboard (first public)
items.summary: 2025: 540 incidents; 10,500 channels/websites mobilised. AI content ‘shifted from experimental use to routine deployment.’ 25% of incidents AI-generated. Strategic shift: supply-chain disruption of FIMI enablers (financial, technical, organisational) replaces reactive detection.
items.asymmetric: Supply-chain deterrence approach could provoke adversary adaptation — increased use of AI-generated content harder to attribute and from infrastructure harder to target.

items.title: Agentic AI in autonomous narrative attacks
items.platform_response: Not documented for narrative-attack use case
items.detection_method: Blackbird.AI narrative intelligence; Anthropic internal detection
items.summary: Blackbird.AI RAV3N 2026: narrative attack costs have ‘collapsed dramatically.’ AI agents autonomously generate and A/B-test propaganda to identify which messages gain traction. 35% of executives cite deepfakes as top concern. Gartner: 40% of social engineering attacks will target executives with deepfake audio/video by 2028.
items.asymmetric: Move from human-directed AI tools to AI agents that autonomously select and optimise information operation tactics represents qualitative threshold change. If narrative attack agents self-optimise faster than defenders adapt detection, current detection-response paradigm becomes structurally obsolete.

items.title: Stanford Internet Observatory dismantled — structural detection gap created
items.detection_method: Indirect — policy outcomes, researcher pressure campaigns
items.summary: SIO dismantled under right-wing political pressure following election misinformation reports. Election research capacity not reconstituted. Represents a successful information operation targeting disinformation research infrastructure itself. Structural monitoring gap for 2026 US midterms.

capability_watch.detail: First confirmed use of AI agents for autonomous breach (Chinese APT, Sep 2025) now documented as directly applicable to narrative manipulation. Self-optimising propaganda at machine speed entering operational FIMI toolkit.

asymmetric_flags: FIMI Deterrence Playbook supply-chain approach may accelerate adversary adaptation to harder-to-detect AI-generated infrastructure.
Stanford Internet Observatory dismantlement is itself a model for future operations targeting disinformation research capacity.
US midterms 2026 proceed without SIO-equivalent monitoring capacity — the structural detection gap materialises at the highest-stakes democratic moment of the year.

module_13.title: AI & Society

items.title: Quinnipiac poll (30 March): AI adoption rising, trust falling — Gen Z most pessimistic
items.summary: 73% use AI (up from 67% in April 2025). Only 21% trust AI most/all of the time. 76% trust rarely or only sometimes. 55% say AI will do more harm than good. 70% think AI advancements will cut job opportunities (up from 56%). Gen Z: 81% foresee job decreases; only 4% expect net job gains. 66% say government not doing enough to regulate.
items.asymmetric: Trust-adoption gap is the political substrate for the 2026 midterm AI narrative. Candidates running on AI job-destruction themes now have validated polling. Cohort forming political identities around AI scepticism will shape legislative environment through 2030.

items.title: Pew Research: 5-year synthesis — largest expert/public AI optimism gap ever documented
items.affected_groups: US public vs. AI expert community
items.summary: Half of US adults more concerned than excited (up from 37% in 2021). 56% of AI experts think AI will have positive impact vs. only 17% of general public — largest gap in 5 years of Pew data. Americans more open to AI in medical care (44%) than education (24%) or jobs (23%). 21% of workers use AI in jobs (up from 16% in 2024).
items.asymmetric: Expert/public gap this large is structurally dangerous for governance legitimacy. When expert-designed policies diverge from public expectations at this scale, political backlash tends to be non-proportionate and technically uncalibrated.

items.title: NBER WP 34836: 90% of executives report zero AI productivity impact; project 9x job-cut acceleration
items.summary: 6,000 senior executives across US, UK, Germany, Australia surveyed. 69% use AI; 90% report zero employment or productivity impact over 3 years. But project AI will cut employment 0.7% over next 3 years and boost productivity 1.4%. Separate NBER/Duke CFO survey: 502,000 AI-driven job cuts projected in 2026 vs. 55,000 in 2025 (9x acceleration).
items.asymmetric: Non-linear disruption curve: zero past impact + 9x near-future acceleration. Policymakers using this data to justify preemptive regulation will have unusually strong empirical foundation. Political window for structural labour market intervention is 2026 — before the wave hits.

items.title: OECD AI-WIPS 2026: algorithmic management at 89% firm adoption, governance effectiveness uncertain
items.summary: OECD conference (exact window 30 March–1 April) found 89% of firms using algorithmic management tools have at least one governance measure (guidelines, audits). Whether firm-level governance is effective remains uncertain. ITUC represented; no new binding multilateral commitments.

items.title: Children and AI: emerging social consensus for regulation despite broader deregulatory push
items.summary: White House Framework explicitly addresses children’s digital safety and sexual exploitation risk. Multiple state bills target companion chatbots and minor-facing AI. FTC investigation into AI chatbots partly due to child protection. ACM USTPC warned about manipulative potential of chatbots interacting with minors. Children as the one domain where regulatory momentum cuts across the partisan divide.

structural_trends: Expert/public AI optimism gap at 5-year high (Pew): governance legitimacy risk rising.
Trust-adoption divergence in polling: political substrate for 2026 midterm AI backlash narrative is established.
Non-linear labour disruption curve: 9x job-cut acceleration projected while 90% of firms report zero past impact. Disruption is imminent and non-linear.
Children as cross-partisan regulatory consensus: the one AI domain where regulation advances regardless of broader deregulatory trend.
asymmetric_flags: The political window for structural labour market intervention is 2026 — before the non-linear disruption wave hits. Post-wave legislation will be reactive and blunt.

Largest expert/public gap documented in 5 years is the precondition for governance legitimacy crisis: when expert-designed AI policies diverge from public expectations at this scale, political backlash is disproportionate.
Gen Z forming political identities around AI scepticism will reshape the legislative environment through the 2030 census cycle.

module_14.title: AI & Power Structures

items.title: Hyperscaler capex: $700B in 2026; bilateral GPU supply lock-in intensifying
items.summary: $700B hyperscaler data center capex in 2026. AMD-Meta $100B agreement for 6 GW AI capacity; Nvidia-OpenAI $100B GPU-for-stock deal. Amazon ($200B), Google ($175–185B), Meta ($115–135B) guidance. 80% of $2.9T projected through 2028 still ahead. North American data center vacancy ~1%. Deutsche Telekom/Nvidia €1B Munich AI data center opened. Adani $100B India AI infrastructure plan (Google partnership).
items.asymmetric: Bilateral GPU supply lock-in concentrates compute access into hyperscaler relationships. By 2028, energy infrastructure moat (nuclear PPAs, dedicated generation) will be the primary barrier to frontier AI entry — more durable than chip access or model architecture.

items.title: GSA AI clause: procurement as new concentration vector
items.summary: GSA GSAR 552.239-7001 would require ‘American AI Systems’ only, grant irrevocable ‘any lawful’ use rights, bar non-US AI. Creates captive domestic compute/model market for all US government AI procurement.
items.asymmetric: The ‘American AI Systems’ requirement, if enacted, would add a fifth concentration dimension — regulatory capture of AI governance — reinforcing all five existing concentrating dimensions simultaneously.

concentration_index.note: Bilateral GPU supply lock-in (AMD-Meta $100B, Nvidia-OpenAI $100B) intensifying concentration. $700B hyperscaler capex 2026.

module_15.title: Personnel & Org Watch

lab_movements.name: OpenAI — headcount doubling
lab_movements.significance: Scale-up aligned with Pentagon contract, $852B valuation, and IPO preparation. Acquisition of Promptfoo (AI agent security testing tool) in March.
lab_movements.asymmetric: AWS→OpenAI sales talent flow (Rosecrans, VP Strategic Pursuits) signals 2026 strategic priority: commercial and government revenue scale over pure research. Difficult to reverse once embedded.

government_ai_bodies.detail: Adam Beaumont (ex-GCHQ Chief AI Officer) appointed Interim Director October 2025. No permanent director appointment confirmed as of 1 April 2026. Jade Leung (former CTO) now PM’s AI adviser. Oliver Ilott promoted to Interim DG for AI at DSIT. Leadership vacuum approaching August 2026 AI Act enforcement deadline.

government_ai_bodies.body: US NIST CAISI (formerly AI Safety Institute)
government_ai_bodies.detail: NIST AISI renamed Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI). Staff cuts (February 2025) gutted original AISI. Designated by Commerce Secretary Lutnick as industry’s primary government AI testing contact. $20M awarded for two new AI centres with MITRE (January 2026).

government_ai_bodies.status: ACTIVE — no new senior appointments in window
government_ai_bodies.detail: Focal point for GPAI Code of Practice compliance and enforcement from August 2026. No new senior appointments confirmed.

revolving_door.observation: No confirmed AISI pipeline movements (Issue 1 baseline: no confirmed movements); maintained
revolving_door.detail: Institutional rebranding (UK AISI → AI Security Institute; US NIST AISI → CAISI; International Network → INAIMES) is making senior personnel tracking more complex as role descriptions and hierarchies settle.
revolving_door.12_month_implication: All three major AI safety government bodies simultaneously in leadership transition or reduced capacity approaching the August 2026 enforcement threshold. International safety governance coordination vacuum at the most consequential moment since the Paris AI Action Summit.

asymmetric_flags: No confirmed AISI pipeline movements — but the institutional rebranding complexity means the monitoring gap itself is a governance signal.
All three principal AI safety oversight bodies (UK, US, EU) in leadership transition simultaneously approaching August 2026 — coordination vacuum at enforcement cliff.
OpenAI headcount doubling + Rosecrans (AWS→OpenAI VP Strategic Pursuits) signals shift from research lab to government contractor culture.

flags.title: AI-enabled FIMI: capability layer update — agentic autonomous narrative attacks confirmed

The AI Governance Monitor’s capability layer (agentic AI autonomous breach documented by Anthropic, Sep 2025; applied to narrative operations Feb 2026) directly feeds the FIMI monitor’s actor and campaign tracking.
flags.ai_governance_perspective: The capability collapse in narrative attack costs is a direct output of frontier model advances we track in M02 and M12. The FIMI monitor provides attribution and campaign-level context; we provide the underlying capability trajectory that determines how fast those campaigns will evolve.

flags.title: AI deepfakes in 2026 US midterms + Stanford Internet Observatory dismantlement: dual democratic integrity stress
flags.linkage: The AI deepfake capability documented in M12 is now confirmed as active in US midterm campaigns (Reuters, 28 March). Simultaneously, the Stanford Internet Observatory — the primary independent monitoring infrastructure for US election disinformation — was dismantled under political pressure. The AI Governance Monitor tracks both the capability (deepfakes at scale) and the governance vacuum (no disclosure law, SIO gone); the Democratic Integrity monitor tracks the electoral environment they are being deployed into.
flags.ai_governance_perspective: The SIO dismantlement is itself a successful information operation targeting disinformation research infrastructure. The combination of deepfake capability + regulatory vacuum + destroyed monitoring capacity creates the conditions for the first US federal election cycle with unmonitored AI-generated political advertising at scale.

flags.title: Military AI procurement two-tier market + autonomous weapons governance vacuum
flags.linkage: The Anthropic-Pentagon injunction (26 March) formally established that the executive branch can designate AI companies as national security supply chain risks for maintaining ethical use conditions. The OpenAI-Pentagon deal (amended 3 March) established the ‘any lawful use’ template. Sen. Gillibrand’s letter revealed DoD rejected guardrails on autonomous weapons. The AI Governance Monitor tracks the procurement architecture; the Strategic Conflict monitor tracks the operational deployment environment those systems enter.
flags.ai_governance_perspective: Our IHL friction analysis (DoD 3000.09 vs. hypersonic engagement timelines; DoD rejection of autonomous weapons guardrails) maps directly to escalation dynamics the conflict monitor tracks. The governance vacuum we document in M08 and M11 becomes operationally relevant when autonomous systems are deployed in active theatres.

flags.title: Hyperscalers as regulated utilities: AI energy infrastructure meets planetary boundaries
flags.linkage: Ratepayer Protection Pledge + PJM BTM rulemaking + EIA pilot survey = three-vector convergence toward treating hyperscalers as regulated energy utilities in a single quarter. $700B capex in 2026; 80% of $2.9T through 2028 still ahead. The AI Governance Monitor tracks the compute infrastructure decisions; the Environmental Risks monitor tracks whether they are compatible with grid stability, energy transition timelines, and planetary boundary constraints.
flags.ai_governance_perspective: We track who controls compute infrastructure and how energy lock-in creates competitive moats. The Environmental Risks monitor provides the planetary-boundary context. The Ratepayer Pledge converting hyperscalers into utility-style entities is the structural event that connects both monitors: it simultaneously constrains AI infrastructure investment returns and forces engagement with the energy transition.

flags.title: EU AI Act Omnibus 28 April trilogue target: European AI regulatory sovereignty at a deadline
flags.linkage: The Omnibus trilogue (opened 26 March; target 28 April agreement) is a European strategic autonomy event as much as a compliance event. The AI Act is Europe’s primary instrument for asserting AI governance sovereignty; the Omnibus is the mechanism for making that instrument workable. The EU AI Act Omnibus and the Standards Vacuum we track in M09 are directly relevant to the European Strategic Autonomy monitor’s tracking of European independence in technology governance.
flags.ai_governance_perspective: The Standards Vacuum (124 days to deadline, no harmonised standards in OJ) is not just a compliance problem — it is a strategic vulnerability. European companies facing compliance obligations without technical standards are disadvantaged relative to US labs with no equivalent framework. The 28 April deadline is the moment when European regulatory sovereignty either consolidates or fractures.

flags.title: OpenAI $852B valuation at 60x revenue: AI investment concentration and systemic financial risk
flags.linkage: OpenAI’s $122B round at $852B valuation (60x revenue) closed 31 March — the most extreme valuation/revenue ratio in tech history. SoftBank, sovereign wealth funds, and institutional allocators are all now exposed. The AI Governance Monitor tracks the strategic implications of this investment concentration; the Macro Monitor tracks debt, credit, market structure, and systemic risk. If confidence breaks in the AI valuation thesis, the macro implications are now large enough to be systemic.
flags.ai_governance_perspective: We track investment signals for what they reveal about strategic priorities. The retail investor tranche ($3B via banking channels) opening this round marks a potential democratisation of AI concentration risk. The Macro Monitor provides the financial stability framing for what a correction at this scale would mean for the AI infrastructure buildout.

key_judgments.judgment: EU AI Act Standards Vacuum enables compliance arbitrage until August 2026

weekly_brief: The persistent EU AI Act Standards Vacuum defines this weeks signal, with no harmonised standards in the Official Journal ahead of high-risk obligations in August 2026. CEN-CENELEC JTC 21 drafts progress slowly, leaving providers in a compliance limbo that risks fragmented enforcement across Member States and definitional gaps for GPAI models. The first release of the GPAI Code of Practice offers interim transparency guidance, set for full finalisation in Q2 2026, but does little to close the vacuum for high-risk systems. EDPS designation as market surveillance authority for EU institutions adds internal oversight capacity, yet highlights resource strains in the multi-level system comprising national MSAs, the AI Office, and the European AI Board. Risk indicators hold steady, with Standards Vacuum at HIGH due to arbitrage potential, Regulatory Implementation Lag at ELEVATED from coordination challenges, and Global Fragmentation at HIGH as extraterritorial rules test US labs. No new model releases, investments, or science breakthroughs register, underscoring a quiet week focused on regulatory plumbing. Sector penetration remains structural, with prohibited practices banned since February 2025 reshaping societal uses. Power concentrates in EU bodies like the AI Office, while lab postures show Anthropic leading in proactive engagement as GPAI signatory. Governance health composite at 0.45 reflects standards drag, stable absent acute shifts. Jurisdiction matrix flags EU at ELEVATED risk from vacuum, US at HIGH dependency without binding rules. Key judgment: vacuum enables arbitrage until deadline. Watch CEN-CENELEC milestones and GPAI systemic-risk designations for capability-governance friction.

governance_health_composite.note: Standards vacuum drags composite; GPAI code progress offsets marginally
